shortDescription.text: Insecure Direct Object Reference via URL parameter
NoSQL Injection via unsanitized input in $where clause

message.text: Insecure Direct Object Reference: The allocations endpoint reads userId from `req.params` (URL path parameter) instead of `req.session`. Any authenticated user can access another user's allocation data by changing the `:userId` segment in `GET /allocations/:userId`. The commented-out fix confirms this is intentional vulnerable code. An attacker only needs a valid session to enumerate all users' financial allocation data.
Route definition accepting arbitrary userId in URL path

properties.impact: Any authenticated user can read any other user's financial allocation data by manipulating the userId URL parameter. No authorization check verifies the requested userId matches the session user.
properties.remediation: Replace `req.params.userId` with `req.session.userId` to ensure users can only access their own allocations.

message.text: NoSQL Injection via the `threshold` query parameter. The value is interpolated directly into a MongoDB `$where` JavaScript expression without sanitization. An attacker can inject arbitrary JavaScript that executes server-side in the MongoDB engine. Example payloads: `0';while(true){}' ` (DoS) or `1'; return 1 == '1` (auth bypass to dump all records). The `$where` operator evaluates the string as JavaScript, making string interpolation equivalent to eval injection.
Unsanitized threshold parameter read from query string

properties.impact: Remote code execution within the MongoDB JavaScript engine. Attacker can exfiltrate all database records, cause denial of service via infinite loops, or bypass query logic to access unauthorized data.
properties.remediation: Parse threshold as an integer with parseInt() and validate the range. Replace the $where clause with standard MongoDB query operators (e.g., {userId: parsedUserId, stocks: {$gt: parsedThreshold}}) to avoid JavaScript evaluation entirely.
